items.title: Risk factors for hip dysplasia
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors

  • Breech presentation: the most relevant factor, especially with extended knees, due to the forced position of hip flexion and knee extension that limits acetabular development.
  • Female sex: up to 4-5 times more frequent due to greater ligamentous laxity induced by maternal hormones (relaxin).
  • Oligohydramnios, primigravidae and family history of hip dysplasia also increase the risk.
  • Macrosomia and congenital torticollis are associated with a higher incidence.

items.title: Graf ultrasound classification
items.contentHtml:
Graf Classification

  • It is based on the measurement of the alpha angle (formed by the bony roof of the acetabulum and the iliac line) and the beta angle (formed by the cartilaginous roof and the iliac line).
  • Type I (normal): alpha ≥60°, beta <55°; mature hip.
  • Type IIa (physiological immature <3 months): alpha 50-59°, beta >55°; requires control.
  • Type IIb (≥3 months): alpha 50-59°, beta >55°; dysplasia requiring treatment.
  • Type III (dislocated): alpha <50°, beta >77°; displaced femoral head with compressed cartilaginous roof.
  • Type IV (severely dislocated): alpha <50°, beta not measurable; dislocated femoral head with inverted cartilage.

items.title: Ortolani maneuver
items.contentHtml:
Ortolani maneuver

  • Ortolani maneuver: detects a dislocated hip that is reducible. The examiner flexes the hip to 90° and performs a gentle abduction while applying anterior pressure with the fingers on the greater trochanter.
  • A “click” or “pop” is perceived when the femoral head enters the acetabulum.
  • It is positive only in infants younger than 2-3 months; Later, muscle contracture prevents passive reduction.
  • A negative Ortolani maneuver does not rule out dysplasia, especially if there is fixed dislocation.

items.title: Barlow maneuver
items.contentHtml:
Barlow maneuver

  • Barlow maneuver: seeks to cause a dislocation in an unstable but still centered hip. With the hip adducted and flexed to 90°, gentle posterior pressure is applied to the knee in an attempt to displace the femoral head out of the acetabulum.
  • A “pop” is felt upon dislocation, and when the pressure is released, the head usually reduces spontaneously (which may also be perceived as a second pop).
  • A positive Barlow maneuver indicates dislocatable instability and requires ultrasound confirmation.
  • Both maneuvers (Ortolani and Barlow) are complementary; Their sensitivity decreases after 6-8 weeks of life.

items.foco: Asymmetry of gluteal and femoral folds
items.title: Fold asymmetry
items.contentHtml:
Asymmetry of gluteal and femoral folds

  • asymmetry of gluteal and femoral folds is a non-specific but classic clinical sign in unilateral hip dysplasia.
  • A greater number or depth of folds is observed in the thigh and gluteus on the affected side, due to proximal displacement of the femur.
  • It is not diagnostic on its own: up to 30% of healthy infants may have fold asymmetry, and many children with confirmed dysplasia do not have it.
  • If it is associated with other signs (limitation of abduction, Galeazzi sign), clinical suspicion increases.

items.foco: Galeazzi sign (apparent shortening of the femur)
items.title: Galeazzi sign
items.contentHtml:
Galeazzi sign

  • Galeazzi sign (or Allis sign): evaluates the difference in knee height when the infant is supine with hips and knees flexed at 90° and feet resting on the table.
  • In a unilateral dislocation, the knee on the affected side is lower (apparent shortening of the femur) due to proximal displacement of the femoral head.
  • It is a sign of established dislocation; It is not usually positive in mild dysplasias or instabilities without frank dislocation.
  • It can also be positive in other causes of femoral shortening (agenesis, fracture), so it should always be complemented with imaging.

items.title: Limitation of abduction
items.contentHtml:
Limitation of hip abduction

  • Limitation of hip abduction is the most reliable clinical sign after 3 months of age, when the Ortolani and Barlow maneuvers lose sensitivity.
  • It is explored with the infant in the supine position, hips and knees flexed at 90°, abducting both hips simultaneously. Normally 70-80° is reached; an asymmetric abduction (<60° on one side) is suspicious.
  • The limitation is due to contracture of the adductors and displacement of the femoral head.
  • It is a sign of fixed dislocation or severe dysplasia; may be present in bilateral dysplasia (symmetrical limited abduction).

items.foco: Ultrasound as gold standard before 6 months
items.title: Ultrasound in hip dysplasia
items.contentHtml:
Ultrasound as gold standard before 6 months

  • Hip ultrasound is the method of choice for the diagnosis and monitoring of developmental dysplasia of the hip in infants under 6 months, before the ossification nucleus of the femoral head appears (generally between 4-6 months).
  • It allows the evaluation of acetabular morphology (alpha and beta angle of Graf), femoral coverage and dynamic stability.
  • It is performed systematically in countries with universal screening (e.g. Austria, Germany) or directed at risk factors.
  • After 6 months, simple radiography (anteroposterior projection of the pelvis) is more reliable, assessing the acetabular index and the Hilgenreiner-Perkins line.

items.title: Pavlik harness treatment
items.contentHtml:
Pavlik harness as initial treatment

  • The Pavlik harness is the first-line treatment for hip dysplasia in infants younger than 6 months (Graf IIb, III and IV, or clinical instability).
  • It maintains the hips in flexion (90-110°) and limited abduction (not >60°) to allow spontaneous reduction and development acetabular.
  • It is used 24 hours a day for 6-12 weeks, with serial ultrasound controls every 1-2 weeks to verify reduction.
  • Contraindications: irreducible dislocation, femoral fracture, brachial palsy; requires strict supervision to avoid avascular necrosis of the femoral head due to excessive abduction (>60°).

items.foco: Harness failure: closed or open reduction
items.title: Pavlik harness failure
items.contentHtml:
Failure of the harness: closed or open reduction

  • It is considered failure of the Pavlik harness when after 3-4 weeks of use the reduction is not achieved or ultrasound instability persists.
  • In this case, closed reduction under general anesthesia is indicated with arthrography to confirm the concentric position, followed by immobilization with spica cast in “human position” position (flexion 90-100°, abduction 40-50°).
  • If closed reduction fails (due to interposition of the labrum or hypertrophied round ligament), open reduction is required (anterolateral or medial route) and, depending on age, a pelvic or femoral osteotomy.
  • The goal is to achieve a stable concentric reduction before 12-18 months to optimize acetabular development.

items.foco: Complications: avascular necrosis of the femoral head
items.title: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
items.contentHtml:
Complications: avascular necrosis of the femoral head

  • Avascular necrosis of the femoral head (AVN) is the most serious complication of the treatment of hip dysplasia, with an incidence of 0-30% depending on the technique and age.
  • It is caused by compression of the medial femoral circumflex artery during immobilization in forced abduction (>60°) or due to traumatic reduction.
  • Clinically it can manifest as femoral shortening, coxa vara or deformity of the femoral head (flattening, irregularity).
  • To prevent it, it is recommended to maintain abduction <60° in the Pavlik harness and avoid hyperabduction in the spica cast; Radiological follow-up is essential until skeletal maturity.

items.foco: Post-treatment radiological follow-up until skeletal maturity
items.title: Post-treatment radiological follow-up
items.contentHtml:
Post-treatment radiological follow-up until skeletal maturity

  • All children treated for hip dysplasia require radiological follow-up until skeletal maturity (closure of the growth physes, around 14-16 years) to detect residual dysplasia or late avascular necrosis.
  • Anteroposterior radiographs of the pelvis are performed. regular intervals: at one year, at 2 years, at 4-5 years and at the beginning of puberty, or according to the specialist's criteria.
  • The acetabular index is evaluated (normal <30° at one year, <25° at 2 years), the Sharp angle in adults and the coverage of the femoral head (line of Wiberg).
  • Early detection of residual dysplasia allows pelvic osteotomies (Salter, Pemberton, Ganz) to be indicated before early osteoarthritis develops.
